From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Team representing England in international rugby league

This article is about the men's team. For the women's team, see [England women's national rugby league team](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_rugby_league_team "England women's national rugby league team").

Not to be confused with [England national rugby union team](/wiki/England_national_rugby_union_team "England national rugby union team") or [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team").

The **England national rugby league team** represents England in international [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") since the first international match in 1904. It is controlled by the [Rugby Football League](/wiki/Rugby_Football_League "Rugby Football League") (RFL), the governing body for rugby league in England, and participated in the [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") and other test matches.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The England national team is the world's oldest national rugby league team, having played the first international rugby league game in 1904 against [Other Nationalities](/wiki/Other_Nationalities "Other Nationalities"),[[a]](#cite_note-2) a team initially made up of Welsh and Scottish players.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

England have made the Rugby League World Cup final three times, being runners-up; in [1975](/wiki/1975_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1975 Rugby League World Cup"), [1995](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup") and [2017](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup").

The head coach role is vacant after [Shaun Wane](/wiki/Shaun_Wane "Shaun Wane") resigned in January 2026.[[3]](#cite_note-4) [George Williams](/wiki/George_Williams_(rugby_league,_born_1994) "George Williams (rugby league, born 1994)") is the captain.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1904–1995: Foundations and European Championships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1904–1995: Foundations and European Championships")]

For most of its history, England has been represented by the [Great Britain national rugby league team](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") in [tours](/wiki/List_of_rugby_league_tours "List of rugby league tours") and [world cups](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup"). The only competitive exception to this was the [European Championship](/wiki/Rugby_League_European_Championship "Rugby League European Championship"). England, in its early years, would also play regular friendlies against [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") to ensure the strength of the Great Britain side. The first international rugby league game was played by England in 1904.

At the start of 1903–04 season the Northern Union thought about international matches and scheduled a match for England on New Year's Day 1904 in [Oldham](/wiki/Oldham "Oldham"). On that day though, the ground was frosty and the match was cancelled and it was rescheduled for April.

On 5 April 1904 England competed against a team called "[Other Nationalities](/wiki/Other_Nationalities_rugby_league_team "Other Nationalities rugby league team")", who were made up of ten Welshman and two Scotsman, including [George Frater](/wiki/George_Frater "George Frater"), who captained the side. It was a period of experimentation for the Northern Union and each team had twelve players, not thirteen. At [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park_(Wigan) "Central Park (Wigan)"), [Wigan](/wiki/Wigan "Wigan") the ground was muddy and in poor condition, however the match went ahead. England steamed into a 3–0 lead, from a try by [Warrington's](/wiki/Warrington_Wolves "Warrington Wolves") [Jackie Fish](/wiki/Jack_Fish_(rugby_league) "Jack Fish (rugby league)"). This is despite [Salford's](/wiki/Salford_Red_Devils "Salford Red Devils") [James Lomas](/wiki/James_Lomas_(rugby_league) "James Lomas (rugby league)") arriving late and causing England to start the match with eleven players. Fish missed the conversion and so the Other Nationalities were able to level the scores a little later, Welshman Thomas crashing over for a try. The conversion was missed and going into half-time the score was tied 3–3. In the second half Thomas went over for another try before [Wigan's](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors") Harris sealed a 9–3 win for the Other Nationalities in the final minutes of the match. A total of 6,000 spectators turned up for the match, which was considered a poor showing despite a [Broughton Rangers](/wiki/Broughton_Rangers "Broughton Rangers") v Bradford cup clash being scheduled on the same day.

In 1905 a match between the two sides was played at Bradford. This time England won 26–11 even though they were losing 11–0 at half-time. Wigan's [Jim Leytham](/wiki/Jim_Leytham "Jim Leytham") scored four tries in succession, a record that still stand today.[[4]](#cite_note-5) The match was played with fifteen players on each side and so was the 1906 match. Played in Wigan again, the match finished a 3–3 draw. The concept was abandoned after the 1906 match. By 1908 the game had expanded much more into Australia, New Zealand and Wales and England began playing those teams. [Harold Wagstaff](/wiki/Harold_Wagstaff "Harold Wagstaff") made his debut for England in 1908 against the touring Kangaroos team at 17 years and 228 days.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

The Other Nationalities side did return in 1921. An England side beat the [Australasian](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia") team of the [1921–22 Kangaroo tour of Great Britain](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_Kangaroo_tour_of_Great_Britain "1921–22 Kangaroo tour of Great Britain") 4–5 at [Highbury](/wiki/Arsenal_Stadium "Arsenal Stadium"). England played only one international between 10 May 1956 and 7 November 1968 an 18–6 victory at [Headingley Rugby Stadium](/wiki/Headingley_Rugby_Stadium "Headingley Rugby Stadium"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds").

### 1975 "One-off" World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1975 \"One-off\" World Cup")]

England played at the [World Cup in 1975](/wiki/1975_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1975 Rugby League World Cup") coached by [Alex Murphy](/wiki/Alex_Murphy_(rugby_league) "Alex Murphy (rugby league)"), which was played over several months in both hemispheres on a league basis. Normally [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") would represent England in the World Cup, but the RLIF wanted to capitalise on the large number of Welsh players in the game at the time, and so England and Wales fielded separate teams.

England won their first match, a 20–2 victory over France in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds") in March. In June the Lions suffered their first defeat in just their second match of the tournament, losing 12–7 against a strong Wales side in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"). A little later England managed to hold on for a draw against Australia in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), the final score being 10–10. And they also picked up a point in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), drawing 17–17 against New Zealand. At the end of October, after the domestic season had finished, England beat the Welsh 22–16 in [Warrington](/wiki/Warrington "Warrington") and then crossed the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to thrash a French side 48–2 in [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"). Bradford played host the England versus New Zealand match, in which England won comfortably 27–12.

At the start of November, England squeezed past Australia winning 16–13 in November at Wigan. This meant that the Kangaroos had finished on 13 points, with the Lions on 12 points. Australia were deemed champions by finishing top of the table, but because they had not beaten England a final match was quickly arranged. Australia beat England 25–0 at Leeds to clinch their fourth title.

### 1995–2007: Regular World Cup appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1995–2007: Regular World Cup appearances")]

#### 1995 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1995 World Cup")]

With the break up of the Great Britain team into its individual nations, England (as co-host) were in the [1995 World Cup](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup"), their first appearance in the World Cup since 1975. England were coached by [Phil Larder](/wiki/Phil_Larder "Phil Larder"). The Lions got off to a flying start beating Australia 20–16 in the opening game at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"), then hammering [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_national_rugby_league_team "Fiji national rugby league team") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa_national_rugby_league_team "South Africa national rugby league team") in the remaining group games to finish top of group A. This set up a semi-final game at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") against Wales. England won the tussle 25–10 to reach the World Cup final, but they lost 16–8 to Australia at Wembley Stadium. England would not play again until 2000.

The 1995 World Cup saw the first change of the England strip in a number of years. Instead of the usual all-white kit, an offset red St George's Cross was added to both the front and back of the jumper.

#### 2000 World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 World Cup")]

[John Kear](/wiki/John_Kear "John Kear") was coach of England for the [World Cup in 2000](/wiki/2000_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2000 Rugby League World Cup"). Compared to 1995, England had little success, losing their opening game at [Twickenham](/wiki/Twickenham_Stadium "Twickenham Stadium") 22–2 against [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team"). But they won their remaining two pool games against [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji_national_rugby_league_team "Fiji national rugby league team") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_rugby_league_team "Russia national rugby league team"). A surprisingly competitive display by [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_league_team "Ireland national rugby league team") in the quarter-finals, saw England scrape through to the semi-finals 26–16. England then went down to a record defeat, losing 49–6 to [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") at [Bolton](/wiki/Bolton "Bolton"), and were knocked out of the tournament.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

### 2007–present: Break up of Great Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2007–present: Break up of Great Britain")]

Following the [2007 All Golds Tour](/wiki/2007_All_Golds_Tour "2007 All Golds Tour"), Great Britain splits into separate [home nations](/wiki/Home_nations "Home nations") teams meaning England would represent itself in all international rugby league competitions.

#### 2008–2009: Tony Smith era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2008–2009: Tony Smith era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/England_2008_RLWC.jpg/960px-England_2008_RLWC.jpg)](/wiki/File:England_2008_RLWC.jpg "England at the 2008 World Cup")

England at the [2008 World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup")

Australian born, [Tony Smith](/wiki/Tony_Smith_(rugby_league,_born_1967) "Tony Smith (rugby league, born 1967)"), took charge of England in 2008. His first game was against France in Toulouse where the English won 56–8. In his second game, England were missing St Helens and Leeds Rhinos players but the team still created history with a record 74–0 win over Wales in Doncaster. It was England's biggest win recorded over the Welsh since 1978.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

It was [World Cup year](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup"), and Smith announced his ambitions that he wanted England to win their first World Cup, since 1972, when Great Britain represented the country at the event. In the event they were placed in Group A alongside hosts Australia, New Zealand and Papua New Guinea. England faced a scare in their opening game against Papua New Guinea, as Smith's men were trailing 12–16 at halftime, but they did go on to win the game. England were humiliated in their second game against the hosts, suffering their biggest defeat to date, beating their 43-point margin against New Zealand eight years ago.[[8]](#cite_note-9) In their final pool game against New Zealand, they produced a much better performance but for only 28 minutes, as they gave up a 24–8 lead to lose 24–36. Before, and during the match, England were accusing New Zealand of being soft, however, after the match, media outlets were calling England the biggest losers of the tournament. Controversy also occurred before the game kicked off, when England refused to face New Zealand's haka challenge. Smith said 'In some cultures poking your tongue out at others could be seen as insulting and the Kiwis pushed things too far by crossing into England's side of halfway.'[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) They took on New Zealand again in the semi-final. This time they never had the lead over the Kiwis, as they lost the match by 10 points. After rumours Smith would be sacked from his position, the RFL announced they'd keep faith in Smith for the remainder of his contract. The World Cup players took the blame for their performances.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

After the World Cup nightmare, England began 2009 on a high-note with a record-breaking 54-point away win over France.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Later that year, England were co-hosts of the year's major international tournament, the inaugural [Four Nations](/wiki/2009_Four_Nations "2009 Four Nations"). After thrashing them earlier in the year, England faced a shock half-time deficit in their opening game against a French side coached by former Great Britain international [Bobbie Goulding](/wiki/Bobbie_Goulding "Bobbie Goulding"). But despite trailing at the interval, England scored 30 consecutive points to record another victory over 'Les Tricolores'. In the second match against Australia, England impressively kept Australia scoreless in the second half, and staged a second-half comeback. However it wasn't enough, as Smith's men needed to recover from a 26-point half-time deficit. They then took on New Zealand and, after losing to them twice at last year's World Cup, England earned revenge with an 8-point win over the Kiwis. England then went on to make the final to face Australia. In the final England, at one point, led 16–14 and were credited with how they were able to produce a real contest. However, in the final quarter of the game, Australia dominated proceedings and eventually went on to win 46–16. On 16 November 2009, a few hours after crediting that the England national team had a bright future in rugby league, Smith resigned from the English national side.

#### 2010–2015: Steve McNamara era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010–2015: Steve McNamara era")]

Following Tony Smiths resignation, former [Bradford Bulls](/wiki/Bradford_Bulls "Bradford Bulls") head coach [Steve McNamara](/wiki/Steve_McNamara "Steve McNamara") was given the job. His first game in charge of England was against France in Leigh. England thrashed the French to keep their impressive winning run going over their opponents that dates back to 1981. McNamara also fielded the first brothers, [Sam](/wiki/Sam_Tomkins "Sam Tomkins") and [Joel Tomkins](/wiki/Joel_Tomkins "Joel Tomkins"), to start on the field for England since [Paul](/wiki/Paul_Hulme "Paul Hulme") and [David Hulme](/wiki/David_Hulme_(rugby_league) "David Hulme (rugby league)") represented Great Britain in 1989.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The following year, England co-hosted the [2011 Four Nations](/wiki/2011_Rugby_League_Four_Nations "2011 Rugby League Four Nations") with Wales. Their opening game was a win against Wales where Sam Tomkins scored a record-equalling four tries in one game. The win meant England kept their impressive record of not losing on home soil against Wales since 1977. A loss the following week to Australia at Wembley meant that they had to beat New Zealand to make the final, which they did. In the final, England at one point were tied at 8–8, but they would be outclassed again. England were held 'try-less' in the second half as Australia won by 22 points.

In mid-2012, the second [International Origin series](/wiki/2012_International_Origin_series "2012 International Origin series") was held. The Exiles had won the first series in 2011 after Samoan International, George Carmont, scored a try with less than 40 seconds remaining to win the game for the Exiles. England won their first ever International Origin series game, after winning game 1 of the 2012 series, held in St Helens, by 8 points. However the Exiles would win the 2012 series after recording a bigger winning margin in game 2. In October and November that year, England competed in the [Autumn Internationals](/wiki/2012_Autumn_International_Series "2012 Autumn International Series") where they took on Wales, and France. In their first game, England racked up their biggest points tally against Wales, as they thrashed 'the Red Dragons' 80–12 in Wrexham.[[14]](#cite_note-15) In their second game against France, fullback Sam Tomkins became England's top try scorer when he scored his 14th try for England, breaking the record set by former Wigan and St Helens winger [Alf Ellaby](/wiki/Alf_Ellaby "Alf Ellaby") in 1935.[[15]](#cite_note-16) In the final, England had a rematch with France at Salford City Stadium. England thrashed their opponents to win their first tournament title since the 2004 European Nations Cup.

In the lone [2013 International Origin](/wiki/2013_International_Origin_series "2013 International Origin series") game, England thrashed their opponents by 20 points. At the end of the year, the [2013 World Cup](/wiki/2013_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2013 Rugby League World Cup") was held in England and Wales. England, who were now known as the 'Wall of White', featured a new record of three brothers in their squad: [Sam](/wiki/Sam_Burgess "Sam Burgess") and twins [George](/wiki/George_Burgess_(rugby_league) "George Burgess (rugby league)") and [Tom Burgess](/wiki/Tom_Burgess_(rugby_league) "Tom Burgess (rugby league)"). England played their first game against Australia in Cardiff. England got off to a surprising early lead for many, when they were up 10–0 after 20 minutes. England, however, went on to lose in what was one of their best displays against the Kangaroos in years, losing 20–28. They then went on to thrash Ireland to nil in front of a record crowd in Huddersfield, in a game which saw [Ryan Hall](/wiki/Ryan_Hall_(rugby_league) "Ryan Hall (rugby league)") become the new England top try-scorer after a hat-trick took him to tally 17 total tries for his country. England also beat a determined Fiji, in front a sold-out crowd at the KC Stadium, to advance to the quarter-finals. They took on European rivals, France, in Wigan and, after trailing 0–6 early, England went on to advance to the semi-finals to meet defending World Champions, New Zealand, at Wembley. The game was a see-saw affair, which saw England leading 18–14 with one minute on the clock remaining, until New Zealand play-maker Shaun Johnson produced a historical moment, to level the scores, and then convert his try after the siren, to win the match, and make the Kiwis advance to a third consecutive World Cup final. This was the first time England, or Great Britain, had lost to New Zealand in England since 2005.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In October and November 2014, England travelled down-under to play in the [2014 Four Nations](/wiki/2014_Rugby_League_Four_Nations "2014 Rugby League Four Nations"). In the opening game, England took on Samoa in an affair which saw the lead change several times. In the end, England survived a shock result occurring after winning by 6 points. In the second game against Australia, controversy occurred. Australia led 16–12 with one minute left on the clock. England player, [Liam Farrell](/wiki/Liam_Farrell "Liam Farrell"), put a grubber-kick in the in-goal area, which forced Australian fullback, Greg Inglis, to force the ball dead, however, the video referees decided to have a look and see whether or not Inglis or the incoming Ryan Hall got the last touch on the ball. On the slow-motion replays, it showed that Ryan Hall's right hand's little finger had put some downward pressure on the ball, however, in normal speed, it was deemed 'inconclusive' by Australian officiating rules. It was eventually given a no-try to the anger of English players and fans. Ryan complained on Twitter, saying "Looking at the video, I’d say it was a try if we’re playing Super League rules..." Had Hall scored, and England converted, it would have been England's first win over Australia since 1995, the first time Australia suffered back-to-back home defeats since 1970 and the first time Australia did not qualify for a tournament final since the 1954 World Cup final.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) England lost their final game against New Zealand and, in the process, ended any chance of qualifying for their first Four Nations final in the Southern Hemisphere.

In 2015, England took on New Zealand in a [three match series](/wiki/2015_New_Zealand_rugby_league_tour_of_Great_Britain "2015 New Zealand rugby league tour of Great Britain") held in England. Before the series, England recorded their biggest ever win over France, beating their previous 73–6 win in 1996.[[19]](#cite_note-20) England beat New Zealand 2–1 in the Baskerville Series to retain the trophy that Great Britain last won in 2007.[[20]](#cite_note-21) During that series vice-captain, [James Graham](/wiki/James_Graham_(rugby_league) "James Graham (rugby league)"), reached the milestone of becoming England's most capped player, surpassing [Kevin Sinfield](/wiki/Kevin_Sinfield "Kevin Sinfield")'s record of 27 test appearances.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Despite the series victory, McNamara was facing scrutiny beforehand and the RFL decided not to renew his contract which expired after the series. McNamara therefore left the England national team.

#### 2016–2019: Wayne Bennett era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2016–2019: Wayne Bennett era")]

Following McNamara's contract expiration, the RFL appointed Australian [Wayne Bennett](/wiki/Wayne_Bennett_(rugby_league) "Wayne Bennett (rugby league)") on a 2-year contract, with a view to win the 2017 World Cup. Bennett's first request as coach was for England to have a pre-season training camp, a mid-season international in 2017, as well as shortening the 2017 Super League season in order to prepare for the [2017 World Cup](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup"). RFL Chief Executive Nigel Wood accepted Bennett's first two demands, but said shortening the domestic season is a "big disturbance in lots of ways..."[[22]](#cite_note-23) Bennett's plans suffered criticism from some professional club coaches.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Bennett's first [match](/wiki/2016_Rugby_League_Four_Nations#France_vs_England "2016 Rugby League Four Nations") was against France in Perpignan, in preparation for the [2016 Four Nations](/wiki/2016_Rugby_League_Four_Nations "2016 Rugby League Four Nations"). Despite trailing, and being held scoreless in the first 26 minutes, England did go on to avoid an upset, convincingly beating the French and giving Bennett a successful return to the International scene, 8 years after he left the New Zealand assistant coach role. However England went onto having a disappointing Four Nations, losing the opening game to New Zealand, facing a scare against Scotland, before going on to lose a 'must-win' game against Australia. This marked the first time that England failed to qualify for a Four Nations final while being hosts.

At the end of 2016, Bennett announced that the England pre-season 'heat training camp' in Dubai would be cancelled after taking in the consideration of domestic coaches' concerns.[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) Instead of a pre-season training camp, Bennett and the English coaching staff chose an Elite Performance Squad of players, based in England, who would be scouted regularly throughout the 2017 season. The squad trained six times throughout the season in England.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

England's first game of 2017 was a convincing result over Samoa, although Bennett came under criticism of his selections for the [test-match](/wiki/2017_Pacific_Rugby_League_Tests#England_vs_Samoa "2017 Pacific Rugby League Tests"). Former Great Britain captain [Garry Schofield](/wiki/Garry_Schofield "Garry Schofield"), along with other former players, media, and fans were angered by Bennett's decision to include Australian born players [Chris McQueen](/wiki/Chris_McQueen "Chris McQueen") and [Chris Heighington](/wiki/Chris_Heighington "Chris Heighington"), who are eligible to play through their English fathers. Heighington's age was also a talking point. At the age of 35, it was suggested younger players such as [Liam Farrell](/wiki/Liam_Farrell "Liam Farrell"), [Alex Walmsley](/wiki/Alex_Walmsley "Alex Walmsley"), [Mark Percival](/wiki/Mark_Percival "Mark Percival"), and [Scott Taylor](/wiki/Scott_Taylor_(rugby_league) "Scott Taylor (rugby league)"), should have been selected instead.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Bennett only named one NRL player, Chris Heighnington, in his England team for the World Cup, while [Zak Hardaker](/wiki/Zak_Hardaker "Zak Hardaker") missed out due to a drugs ban. England's first game of the World Cup was against Australia. Although they were credited throughout the match for the way they were able to defend and prevent the Australians from dominating the game like they have in the past they again failed to beat Australia. Despite the defeat, England continued their way through the tournament with convincing victories over Lebanon, France, finishing second in Group A. They then went on to beat Papua New Guinea convincingly in the quarter finals before reaching the semi-finals where they would take on Tonga who had knocked New Zealand out the week before. England led 20–0, with seven minutes left on the clock, before the Tongans began an unpredictable and nerve-wracking comeback for England. With less than one minute left on the clock, Andrew Fifita lost control of the ball before regathering it and putting it over the try-line, only to realise the referee had already blown his whistle. The referee was criticised for not going to the Video Referee and if awarded would have seen that Tonga won the game and reached their first ever World Cup final.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Despite the controversy, England were victorious and had advanced to their first World Cup final in 22 Years.[[30]](#cite_note-31) England met Australia again in the [final](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup_final "2017 Rugby League World Cup final") but again failed to beat Australia, for a 13th consecutive time, since their last win in the 1995 World Cup group stage. England lost in the lowest World Cup final score in the history of the tournament.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Bennets contract expired at the end of the World Cup and despite not winning the tournament he was rewarded with another 2-year contract.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

#### 2020–present: Shaun Wane era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020–present: Shaun Wane era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/England_%40St_James_Park.jpg/250px-England_%40St_James_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:England_@St_James_Park.jpg)

England fans create a [St George's Cross](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") ahead of the opening matches of the [2021 Men's Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2021_Men%27s_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2021 Men's Rugby League World Cup") at [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park")

On 3 February 2020, it was announced that [Shaun Wane](/wiki/Shaun_Wane "Shaun Wane") would replace Bennett as Head Coach on a two-year contract with the intention of leading England in the [2020 Ashes series](/wiki/2020_Kangaroo_tour_of_England "2020 Kangaroo tour of England") and the [2021 World Cup](/wiki/2021_Men%27s_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2021 Men's Rugby League World Cup").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

England started the [2021 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2021_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2021 Rugby League World Cup") with a 60-6 victory over Samoa and finished top of their group after winning all three pool matches. England would then defeat Papua New Guinea to reach the semi-final. In the semi-final, England would lose 27-26 against Samoa during golden point extra-time.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) In October and November 2023, England played in the [2023 Tonga rugby league tour of England](/wiki/2023_Tonga_rugby_league_tour_of_England "2023 Tonga rugby league tour of England"). England won the series 3-0.[[36]](#cite_note-37)
In Autumn 2024, England won a 2-0 Test series victory over Samoa. This marked a significant moment for England following their narrow loss to Samoa in the Rugby League World Cup semi-final the previous year. In the 2025 Ashes Series test against Australia, England would lose 3-0 with England only scoring two tries across the three matches.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

## Identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Identity")]

### Kits and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Kits and colours")]

England traditionally play in white, their early colours were white with red and gold stripes while later they played in white and red hoops. They occasionally play in a red away kit but colour clashes are rare and they rarely wear an away kit.

In the 1975 World Cup they played in a fully white kit. While they predominately played in white and red blue was also occasionally incorporated into the kit. It wasn't until 1995 when the St George’s cross was first integrated into the kit.

Ahead of the 2021 World Cup a radical New England kit was released which was white with navy blue shoulders and cross.

### Badge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Badge")]

* **The Lions crest**

From the 1995 Rugby League World cup, it was used a crest with the [Saint George's Cross](/wiki/Saint_George%27s_Cross "Saint George's Cross"), the [Three Lions Coat of Arms of England](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_England "Coat of arms of England") and [Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose"). It was similar to most other English sporting badges, such as the [England national football team](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") and the [English national cricket team](/wiki/English_national_cricket_team "English national cricket team") which all promote similar attributes. Until the mid 1990s, England simply used a red [lion rampant](/wiki/Lion_(heraldry)#Lions_rampant "Lion (heraldry)") as crest.

* **The Shield Crest**

The new official logo was launched on 6 February 2008 on the rugby league magazine programme [Boots N' All](/wiki/Boots_N%27_All "Boots N' All"). The cross of St George is positioned across a three-dimensional shield within the design. The date "1895" is placed through the centre of the cross, symbolising the birth of rugby league. Many people involved in the sport were consulted throughout the design process, which took a little under a year. The logo was first used for the [2008 World Cup](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup") and was replaced in 2017.

* **Three Lions Cross**

As part of a rebrand across all of the RFL in 2017, a new England crest was introduced. It is a merge of both the shield crest and the old lions crest. It has the St. Georges Cross on the background with three lions in front of it.

### World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: World Cup")]

Main article: [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup")

See also: [England at the Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/England_at_the_Rugby_League_World_Cup "England at the Rugby League World Cup")

England have competed seven times in the [World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup"); in [1975](/wiki/1975_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1975 Rugby League World Cup"), [1995](/wiki/1995_Rugby_League_World_Cup "1995 Rugby League World Cup"), [2000](/wiki/2000_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2000 Rugby League World Cup"), [2008](/wiki/2008_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2008 Rugby League World Cup"), [2013](/wiki/2013_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2013 Rugby League World Cup"), [2017](/wiki/2017_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2017 Rugby League World Cup") and [2021](/wiki/2021_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2021 Rugby League World Cup"). They have never won the competition, though finished runners-up to Australia in 1975, 1995 and 2017. In every other year, [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_national_rugby_league_team "Great Britain national rugby league team") have represented England. Despite having only played in 7 world cups, England have hosted matches in 8 different tournaments, including 3 that took part internationally. England have played 39 games in the world cup; winning 24, drawing 2 and losing 13. Of these losses, only two have been against a team other than [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") or [Australia](/wiki/Australia_national_rugby_league_team "Australia national rugby league team"); [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_league_team "Wales national rugby league team") in 1975 and [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa_national_rugby_league_team "Samoa national rugby league team") in 2021. England lost out on reaching the final in 2013, when New Zealand beat them 20-18 in the semi-final, after [Shaun Johnson](/wiki/Shaun_Johnson "Shaun Johnson") scored a conversion in the last 30 seconds of the game to secure the win. England reached the world cup final in 2017, but were beaten by Australia with a score of just 6–0.

As hosts of the 2021 Rugby League World Cup, and as a team who reached the quarter finals in the previous world cup, England automatically qualified. They again reached the semi-final, but lost 27-26, on a golden point after extra time, to Samoa.

### Four Nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Four Nations")]

Main article: [Rugby League Four Nations](/wiki/Rugby_League_Four_Nations "Rugby League Four Nations")

England replaced Great Britain in competing in the Rugby League Four Nations which replaced the previous Tri Nations tournament. They have been runners up twice, in 2009 and 2011. These two tournaments were hosted by England.

### European Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: European Championship")]

Main article: [Rugby League European Championship](/wiki/Rugby_League_European_Championship "Rugby League European Championship")

England have competed in twenty-six European Nations Cups, the first in 1935. In the past the tournament has been axed and revived many times, and it was stopped for six years because of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). From 1935 to 1949 (minus the war years) England played France and Wales annually, and won the tournament in 1935, 1946, 1947 and 1948. From 1950 to 1956 an Other Nationalities team were added as the fourth team in the competition (except in 1956 when Wales did not field a team). During those years England won in 1950 and 1954. Since then the tournament has run for some seasons, but never for more than five years at a time. But from 1970 to 1996 England won it six out of a possible nine times. In 2003 the tournament was revived and England comfortably won, beating her old rivals plus Scotland, Ireland and Russia. England beat the same opponents to win the cup again in 2004. This was the last time England competed to give the competition more of a level playing field for other teams, however the [England Knights](/wiki/England_Knights "England Knights") competed in 2012.

## England Knights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=England_national_rugby_league_team&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: England Knights")]

Main article: [England Knights](/wiki/England_Knights "England Knights")

In 2011 the [England Knights](/wiki/England "England") were created to serve as a step up for the younger players from their club in view of playing for the 1st team. A squad of players were chosen (below the age of 25) to represent the Knights in a few games. Their first ever game was against France and the Knights came out 38–18 victors.

The Knights won the [2012 European Cup](/wiki/2012_European_Cup_(rugby_league) "2012 European Cup (rugby league)") by beating [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland_national_rugby_league_team "Ireland national rugby league team") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_league_team "Scotland national rugby league team") in a 3-game tournament.
